learningObjective: Discover that the derivative of the accumulation function A(x) equals the original function f(x) - making the deep connection that integration and differentiation are inverse operations
ahaMoment: The slope number of the tangent line on A(x) exactly matches the height number of f(x) at every single position, revealing they are always equal
targetMisconception: The FTC feels like magic - students don't understand WHY differentiation and integration are inverses

miniSpec.title: Drag the Point, Compare Two Graphs
miniSpec.narrative_wrapper: Students see two synchronized graphs stacked vertically: the original function f(x) on top and the accumulation function A(x) below. They drag a point along the x-axis and watch how the slope of A(x) at that point relates to the height of f(x) above it.
miniSpec.manipulated_variable: A vertical line or point dragged horizontally along the x-axis, changing the evaluation position x
miniSpec.primary_visual_feedback: A tangent line appears on the A(x) graph showing its slope at that x-value, while a horizontal line extends from f(x) to show its height; both update as the point moves
miniSpec.goal_structure: Free exploration with optional prompt to 'find three different x-values where you notice the pattern'
miniSpec.causal_chain_exposed: The accumulated area under f from 0 to x (shown as shaded region), the numerical value A(x), the tangent line slope on A(x) (with numerical display), and the value f(x) are all visible simultaneously
miniSpec.trace_strategy: Optional toggle to leave 'ghost' tangent lines at previous positions, allowing students to see the pattern persists across multiple x-values
miniSpec.aha_moment: The slope number of the tangent line on A(x) exactly matches the height number of f(x) at every single position, revealing they are always equal

interactionContract.student_changes: drags x and observes both A(x) and its slope (tangent line) updating
interactionContract.student_sees: a tangent line on A(x) whose slope matches f(x) at that point; the derivative of the area function IS the original function

modelVariables.ftc_xPosition: The x-position where we evaluate everything, student-dragged horizontally
modelVariables.ftc_fValue: derived: f(x) - the height of the speed curve at x
modelVariables.ftc_areaValue: derived: A(x) - the accumulated area under f from a to x
modelVariables.ftc_slopeValue: derived: A'(x) - the slope of the accumulation function at x (numerically approximated derivative)
modelVariables.ftc_showGhosts: boolean: whether to show ghost tangent lines at previous positions (optional feature)
modelVariables.ftc_boundA: frozen: left integration bound (carried over from previous section)
modelVariables.ftc_flowersPlanted: array: x-positions where the student has planted flowers (clicked)

assertions.description: On load, the visualization shows the two graphs but the relationship is not yet obvious - inviting exploration
assertions.visualCheck: Two stacked graphs visible: f(x) on top showing speed curve, A(x) below showing accumulated area. A vertical line marks the current x position. The shaded region shows accumulated area. Tangent line is visible on A(x).

assertions.description: The x-position can be dragged horizontally along the axis
assertions.visualCheck: Vertical line moves to x=4, shaded region expands, A(x) value increases, tangent line on A(x) updates

assertions.description: The slope of A(x) at position x approximately equals f(x) at the same position - the core FTC insight
assertions.visualCheck: The slope readout on the tangent line numerically matches the f(x) height readout above, demonstrating A'(x) = f(x)

assertions.description: Moving x changes the accumulated area A(x)
assertions.visualCheck: Shaded region under f(x) expands as x moves right, A(x) readout increases

assertions.description: The left bound a is frozen from previous section and cannot be changed
assertions.visualCheck: Left bound of shaded region stays fixed at a=0, only right bound moves with x

invariants.description: At every x-position, the slope A'(x) approximately equals f(x) - the FTC relationship
Area under positive function is non-negative
The x-position stays within valid range
